## Summary
Integrated Information Theory (IIT) proposes that consciousness is identical to integrated information (Φ), a mathematical quantity measuring the irreducibility of a system's causal structure. IIT starts from phenomenological axioms and derives physical postulates, predicting which systems are conscious and to what degree.

## Evidence
IIT makes specific predictions: the cerebral cortex should have high Φ while the cerebellum (despite more neurons) should have low Φ, consistent with lesion studies. Perturbational complexity index (PCI), an empirical proxy for Φ, correctly classifies consciousness in brain-injured patients with >90% accuracy.

## Significance
IIT is the most mathematically rigorous theory of consciousness, but faces criticism for its panpsychist implications (any system with Φ > 0 is conscious) and computational intractability of calculating Φ for large systems. The adversarial collaboration with Global Workspace Theory (2023) produced mixed results.
